candidates.name: Greg Abbott
candidates.notes: Three-term governor running for fourth term in 2026. Signed Texas Heartbeat Act (SB8) and constitutional carry. Launched Operation Lone Star border initiative. Signed school voucher legislation. Strong NRA supporter (A+ rating). Signed In God We Trust display law for schools. Paralyzed below waist since 1984 jogging accident.

profile.education: B.A. Finance, University of Texas at Austin (1981); J.D., Vanderbilt University Law School (1984)
profile.career: Texas Supreme Court Justice; Texas Attorney General (2002-2015); Governor since 2015
profile.family: Wife Cecilia Abbott (first Hispanic First Lady of TX); daughter Audrey
profile.background: Attorney and jurist; paralyzed from the waist down by a falling tree at age 26; Texas Supreme Court justice (1996-2001); Attorney General (2002-2015); became 48th governor in 2015.

candidates.name: Dan Patrick
candidates.notes: Three-term Lt. Governor running for fourth in 2026. Evangelical Christian who famously said 'I am a Christian first, a conservative second, and a Republican third.' Former radio talk show host. Presides over TX Senate. Led Senate passage of border security, school choice, bathroom bill, anti-CRT legislation. NRA A+ rated. Strong ally of Gov. Abbott on Operation Lone Star.

profile.education: B.A. English, University of Maryland Baltimore County
profile.career: Radio/TV broadcaster; Texas State Senator (2007-2015); Lt. Governor since 2015; member Fellowship of Christian Athletes and International Bible Society

candidates.name: Ken Paxton
candidates.notes: In runoff with John Cornyn for US Senate. Cornyn 41.8% vs Paxton 40.8% in primary. Runoff May 26, 2026. Not seeking re-election as AG.

profile.education: B.A. Psychology, Baylor University (1985); M.B.A., Baylor University (1986); J.D., University of Virginia School of Law (1991)
profile.career: TX House of Representatives (2003-2013); TX State Senate (2013-2015); Attorney General since 2015; Baylor student body president
profile.family: Wife Angela Paxton (TX State Senator, District 8)

candidates.name: Glenn Hegar
candidates.notes: Served as Comptroller 2015-2025. Departed July 2025 to become Chancellor of Texas A&M University System. Kelly Hancock (former state senator) serving as Acting Comptroller. Office up for election 2026. Hegar implemented anti-ESG investment policies, boycotted companies that discriminate against firearms industry.

profile.education: B.S. Agricultural Economics, Texas A&M University; J.D., University of Arkansas School of Law
profile.career: TX House (2003-2007); TX Senate (2007-2015); Comptroller (2015-2025); now Chancellor of Texas A&M System

candidates.name: Dawn Buckingham
candidates.notes: First-term Land Commissioner (took office Jan 2023), running for re-election 2026. Physician (oculoplastic surgeon). Seventh-generation Texan. As TX State Senator represented SD-24 (2017-2023). Strong pro-life, pro-2A, pro-border security record in Senate. Managing TX public lands, Alamo, Veterans Land Board.

profile.education: B.S. Kinesiology, University of Texas at Austin (1992, Magna Cum Laude); M.D., University of Texas Medical Branch at Galveston (1997)
profile.career: Oculoplastic surgeon; TX State Senator SD-24 (2017-2023); Land Commissioner since 2023
profile.family: Husband Ed Buckingham (also a physician); children

candidates.name: Sid Miller
candidates.notes: Three-term Agriculture Commissioner (since 2015). Lost 2026 Republican primary to Nate Sheets. Remains in office through Jan 2027. Eighth-generation farmer/rancher. Former TX state rep. Outspoken conservative and Trump ally. Known for controversial social media posts. Strong advocate for rural Texas values, school prayer, and traditional agriculture.

profile.education: A.A., Cisco Junior College; B.S. Vocational Agriculture Education, Tarleton State University (1978)
profile.career: Farmer/rancher; rodeo competitor; TX House of Representatives (2001-2013); Agriculture Commissioner since 2015

candidates.name: Ted Cruz
candidates.notes: Junior Senator since 2013. First Hispanic senator from TX. 2016 presidential candidate. Heritage Action lifetime score ~97%. ACU lifetime 97%. FRC Action 100%. NRA A+ rated. Led filibuster against Obamacare. Strong constitutionalist. Argued 9 cases before US Supreme Court as TX Solicitor General. Father Rafael is a pastor.

profile.education: B.A. Public Policy, Princeton University (1992); J.D., Harvard Law School (1995, magna cum laude)
profile.birthplace: Calgary, Alberta, Canada (raised in Houston, TX)
profile.career: Law clerk to Chief Justice Rehnquist; TX Solicitor General (2003-2008); Private law practice; US Senator since 2013; 2016 presidential candidate
profile.family: Wife Heidi Cruz (former Goldman Sachs); two daughters
profile.background: Former Texas Solicitor General and appellate attorney. Clerked for Chief Justice Rehnquist. 2016 presidential candidate. In Senate since 2013.

candidates.name: John Cornyn
candidates.notes: In Republican primary runoff vs Ken Paxton for 2026 re-election. Senate Minority Whip. More moderate/establishment than Cruz.

profile.education: B.A. Journalism, Trinity University (San Antonio); J.D., St. Mary's University School of Law; LL.M., University of Virginia School of Law
profile.birthplace: Houston, Texas (raised in military family, moved frequently)
profile.career: District Court Judge (1985-1991); TX Supreme Court Justice (1991-1997); TX Attorney General (1999-2002); US Senator since 2002; Senate Minority Whip
profile.background: Former Texas Attorney General and Texas Supreme Court Justice. District court judge before that. In Senate since 2002. Former Senate Majority Whip.

candidates.notes: Spring/NW Harris County. Very conservative. Anti-transgender sports bill champion.

candidates.name: Jane Hughson
candidates.notes: First elected mayor 2018, re-elected 2020, 2022, 2024. Previously served on council 1996-2002 and 2014-2018. Longtime San Marcos resident. TX State University retiree (33 years). Named as defendant in TX AG lawsuit over city sanctuary-style policies. Voted for property tax increase. Council under her leadership considered Gaza ceasefire resolution (ultimately denied).

candidates.name: Matthew Mendoza
candidates.notes: Re-elected 2025. Voted against property tax increase. Named as defendant in TX AG lawsuit over city policies. Nonpartisan city election — limited public info on positions.

candidates.name: Josh Paselk
candidates.notes: Newly elected Dec 2025 runoff, defeating incumbent Saul Gonzales with 54.4%. Term 2025-2027. Nonpartisan city election.

candidates.name: Alyssa Garza
candidates.office: City Council, Place 3 (Deputy Mayor Pro Tem)
candidates.notes: Re-elected 2024. Deputy Mayor Pro Tem. Voted FOR property tax increase. Voted to bring Gaza ceasefire resolution to a vote. Named as defendant in TX AG lawsuit over city immigration-related policies. Leans progressive.

candidates.name: Shane Scott
candidates.notes: Mayor Pro Tem. Voted AGAINST property tax increase. Was 'on the fence' on ceasefire resolution. Voted for postponement on data center project. Named in TX AG lawsuit. Appears more moderate than some council members.

candidates.name: Lorenzo Gonzalez
candidates.notes: Voted AGAINST property tax increase. Voted to bring ceasefire resolution to a vote. Mixed signals — fiscally conservative on taxes but progressive on foreign policy resolution. Named in TX AG lawsuit.

candidates.name: Amanda Rodriguez
candidates.notes: Voted FOR property tax increase. Voted to bring ceasefire resolution to a vote. Named in TX AG lawsuit. Leans progressive.

candidates.name: Ruben Becerra
candidates.notes: Serving since 2019, seeking third term. Lifetime Democrat. Chair of 2026 TX Democratic Convention. Active in multiple Democratic organizations. Family owns Gil's Broiler Restaurant and The Manske Roll Bakery in San Marcos. Married to Monica Mendez Becerra.

candidates.name: Debbie Gonzales Ingalsbe
candidates.notes: First elected 1996. First woman and first Latina county commissioner in Hays County history. Father also served as Pct 1 commissioner. Former Deputy Constable (Travis County Sheriff's Academy). Priorities: infrastructure, affordable healthcare, criminal justice reform. Ran unopposed 2024.

candidates.name: Michelle Gutierrez Cohen
candidates.notes: Elected 2022 with 60.85% of vote. 40-year Hays County resident. Doctorate in Management/Organizational Leadership. Ran Hays Latinos United (COVID resources for underserved communities). Democratic precinct chair, campaign manager for 4 campaigns. Running for Hays County Judge in 2026. Term expires 2026.

candidates.name: Morgan Hammer
candidates.notes: Elected 2024 with 52.82%. Small business owner (financial planning and real estate). TX State University McCoy School of Business graduate. Served on church leadership committees. Focuses on community values, clean water, fiscal responsibility, public safety. Precinct 3 is largest unincorporated precinct — Wimberley, Driftwood, outskirts of Kyle/San Marcos.

candidates.name: Walt Smith
candidates.notes: First elected 2018, re-elected. Republican. Represents Dripping Springs, Driftwood, portions of Buda. Running for re-election 2026 in Republican primary.

candidates.name: Kelly Higgins
candidates.notes: Elected 2022 with 53.06%. Progressive DA — does not prosecute simple cannabis possession, will not use county resources to prosecute abortion cases, established mental health courts for nonviolent offenses. Targeted under TX 'rogue prosecutors' removal law. Term expires 2026. Alfonso Salazar (D) won 2026 primary to succeed him.

candidates.name: Anthony Hipolito Jr.
candidates.notes: Elected 2024 with 52.69%. 26 years law enforcement — 23 years Austin PD (retired as Sergeant 2021). Then Hays County Sheriff's Office community outreach. Endorsed by retiring Sheriff Gary Cutler (R). Priorities: recruiting/retaining staff, managing rapid county growth, school safety, fentanyl crisis. First new Hays County sheriff in 15 years.

candidates.name: Clementine Cantu
candidates.notes: Board Vice President. District 5 trustee. Limited public information on political positions — school board races in TX are nonpartisan.

candidates.name: Jessica Cain
candidates.notes: District 1 trustee. Limited public information on political positions.

candidates.name: Margie T. Villalpando
candidates.notes: Board Secretary. District 2 trustee. Term expiring — not seeking re-election. Seat to be filled by appointment (no candidates filed).

candidates.name: Sandra Sepulveda Lopez
candidates.notes: District 3 trustee. Ran unopposed — secured new term through 2029.

candidates.name: Brian Shanks
candidates.notes: District 4 trustee. First elected 2022.

candidates.name: Anne Halsey
candidates.notes: Board President. At-Large trustee. Began serving 2024 (unopposed). Campaign slogan 'Putting kids first.'

candidates.name: Miguel Arredondo
candidates.notes: At-Large trustee. Began serving 2024 (unopposed).

profile.background: Former Texas State Senator (1983-2024), longest-serving member of TX Senate. Moderate Democrat, tough-on-crime stance. Took office Jan 1, 2024 at age 74.
Elected 2019, represents west Houston/Spring Branch area. Conservative-leaning on fiscal issues.
Represents Acres Homes/north Houston area. Progressive Democrat, community activist.

Seat vacated by Abbie Kamin (D) in early 2026 to run for Harris County Attorney. Special election April 4, 2026 headed to runoff between Joe Panzarella and Nick Hellyar. District covers Heights, Montrose, Rice Village, Meyerland.
Represents south-central Houston/Third Ward/MacGregor area. Progressive Democrat, educator background.
Represents Clear Lake/NASA area in southeast Houston. Conservative, engineering background.
Represents southwest Houston/Alief area. Elected 2019, moderate Democrat.
Represents west Houston/Memorial area. Attorney, conservative Republican.
Represents east Houston/Near Northside/Denver Harbor. Progressive Democrat, community organizer.
Represents Gulfton/Sharpstown/southwest Houston. Elected 2023, progressive-leaning.
Represents southwest Houston/Fort Bend area. Moderate to progressive Democrat.
Represents south Houston/Sunnyside/South Park area. Democrat, community development focus.
Elected 2023, Republican at-large member. Business-oriented conservative.
Republican at-large member. Conservative, business community ties.
Republican at-large member. Conservative fiscal policy advocate.
Won November 2025 special election for At-Large Position 4. Democrat, progressive-leaning.
Elected 2019, moderate Democrat. Environmental policy focus.
Took office Jan 1, 2025 after defeating incumbent Kim Ogg in Democratic primary. Former prosecutor, supervised vehicular crimes division. Overhauled intake division. Progressive-leaning prosecutor.
Appointed Aug 14, 2024 by Mayor Whitmire. Second Latino to lead HPD. UHD graduate, career law enforcement. Negotiated major police union contract. 85 ICE referrals in 2025.

Sworn in June 18, 2025 as San Antonio's 69th mayor. Air Force veteran, former Pentagon and intelligence official, former congressional candidate (TX-23). First Asian-American female mayor of a major TX city, first openly lesbian mayor of San Antonio. Progressive.
Represents downtown/near east side San Antonio. Progressive-leaning.
Represents east side San Antonio. Progressive, youngest council member when elected in 2021. First openly gay man on SA council.
Represents south side San Antonio. Moderate to progressive Democrat.
Represents west side San Antonio. Moderate Democrat.
Represents west/northwest side San Antonio. Progressive Democrat, community organizer background.
Youngest council member at 24 years old. Won by only 25 votes in 2025. Represents west side. Progressive.
Represents northwest San Antonio. Moderate to progressive.
Represents north central San Antonio. Moderate to progressive.
Represents north San Antonio/Stone Oak area. More conservative-leaning district, moderate to conservative.
Represents far north San Antonio. More conservative-leaning district, moderate to conservative.

Criminal District Attorney since Jan 1, 2019. Announced he will not seek reelection in June 2025. Progressive prosecutor who expanded diversion programs. Claims highest murder conviction rate in over a decade.

Mayor since 2019. Former TX State Rep (D) who switched to Republican in Sept 2023, making Dallas the largest US city with a GOP mayor. Supports law enforcement, low property taxes, business-friendly policies. Spoke at 2024 RNC.
Elected 2019, represents Oak Cliff area. Progressive Democrat, attorney.
Elected 2021, Mayor Pro Tem. Represents West Dallas/Oak Lawn/Love Field area. Moderate to progressive.
Elected 2023, represents south Dallas. Progressive-leaning.
Elected 2025, represents southeast Dallas/Pleasant Grove. Progressive.
Elected 2019, represents east Dallas/Buckner Terrace. Moderate Democrat.
Elected 2025, represents northwest Dallas. Progressive-leaning.
Elected 2019, represents south Dallas/Fair Park. Progressive Democrat.
Elected 2025, represents north-central Dallas/Lake Highlands. Moderate.
Elected 2019, represents east Dallas/White Rock Lake/Lakewood. Moderate, consensus-builder.
Elected 2023, represents north Dallas/Lake Highlands. Conservative-leaning, business community.
Elected 2025, represents far north Dallas/Preston Hollow. Conservative-leaning.
Elected 2019, represents far north Dallas/Farmers Branch area. Most outspoken conservative on council. Fiscal hawk, pro-public safety.

candidates.name: Gay Donnell Willis
candidates.office: City Council - District 13 (Deputy Mayor Pro Tem)

profile.background: Elected 2021, Deputy Mayor Pro Tem. Represents north Dallas. Conservative, business advocate.
Elected 2021, represents downtown/Uptown/East Dallas. Moderate, urban planning focus.
DA since 2019. Lost March 2026 Democratic primary to Judge Amber Givens. Progressive prosecutor known for declining to prosecute minor theft and marijuana cases. Term ends Dec 31, 2026.
Re-elected Nov 2024 by 30+ points for full 4-year term. Former TX State Senator, Austin mayor from 1997-2001 and again from 2022. Moderate Democrat, business-friendly for Austin standards.
Represents east Austin. Progressive Democrat, community activist. Former Mayor Pro Tem.
Represents southeast Austin/Del Valle area. Progressive Democrat.
Represents south Austin. Progressive Democrat.
Represents north-central Austin. Progressive Democrat, housing reform advocate.
Represents south-central Austin/Zilker area. Progressive Democrat, attorney.
Represents northwest Austin. Democrat, moderate-leaning for Austin.
Represents northeast Austin/Windsor Park. DSA member, furthest left on council. Former congressional candidate who ran against TX-10 Rep Michael McCaul.
Represents southwest Austin/Oak Hill. Progressive Democrat, environmental focus.
Represents central/west Austin. Progressive Democrat, tech industry background.
Represents northwest Austin/Cedar Park fringe. Democrat, moderate-leaning for Austin.

Re-elected Nov 2024 for second term, sworn in Jan 2, 2025. Former public defender and Workers Defense Project director. Progressive prosecutor who expanded restorative justice programs, pre-trial diversion, and expunged 1,000+ records. Strained relationship with Austin PD.

Mayor since 2021, re-elected May 2025 with 66.5% of the vote. Youngest mayor of a major US city when elected at age 37. Republican, business-friendly, pro-growth, supported by conservative establishment. Former chief of staff to prior mayor.
Re-elected May 2025 with 73.7%. Represents north Fort Worth. Conservative-leaning, long-serving council member.
Re-elected May 2025 with 71.6%. Represents southeast Fort Worth. Conservative, public safety focus.
Re-elected May 2025 with 74.5%. Represents southwest Fort Worth/Benbrook area. Conservative, fiscally responsible.

Elected May 2025 with 54.3%, replacing Gyna Bivens. Former chair of Tarrant County Democratic Party. Retired AT&T executive. Represents east Fort Worth. Ran for mayor in 2021 but lost to Mattie Parker.
Won June 7, 2025 runoff against Daryl Davis II. Assumed office June 17, 2025. Replaced Jared Williams who did not seek reelection. Represents southeast/south Fort Worth.
Re-elected May 2025 unopposed. Represents west Fort Worth. Conservative, neighborhood development focus.
Re-elected May 2025 with 74.1%. Represents far north Fort Worth/Alliance area. Conservative, pro-growth, public safety advocate.
Re-elected May 2025 with 65.6%. Represents south/southwest Fort Worth. Moderate conservative.
Re-elected May 2025 unopposed. Represents far northwest Fort Worth. Conservative.
Re-elected May 2025 with 77.1%. Represents northeast Fort Worth/Haltom City area. Moderate, community-focused.

Tarrant County Criminal DA since Jan 1, 2023. Former judge of County Criminal Court No. 10 for 25 years. Running for re-election. Tough-on-crime philosophy, traditional prosecution approach. Achieved first TX jury conviction under new state law in 2025.
